mnemonics.title: BOOT Process Sequence
mnemonics.content: BOOTSTRAP: BIOS POST LOAD KERNEL START SERVICES LOGIN - Basic Input Output System → Power On Self Test → Bootstrap Load → Kernel Load → System Services Start → Login Prompt

mnemonics.title: OS Functions
mnemonics.content: SPDM: Storage Management, Process Management, Device Management, Memory Management - remember as SYSTEM PROVIDES DYNAMIC MULTITASKING

mnemonics.title: Dual Mode Protection
mnemonics.content: USER CANT KILL PRIVILEGED: User mode Cannot Access or Touch Kernel instructions - remember as USUALLY ONLY USER CODE, KERNEL IS PROTECTED

acronyms.usage: The main software that manages computer hardware and provides services to applications
Firmware that initializes hardware during the boot process
Allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention
Diagnostic test run by BIOS when computer is powered on
Set of definitions and protocols for building software applications, including system calls
Hardware component that handles memory access requests and virtual memory translation

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pyramid: Top (smallest, fastest) = CPU Registers, Below = Cache, Below = Main Memory, Bottom (largest, slowest) = Magnetic Disk. Think of it like a desk: register is what's in your hand, cache is what's on desk, memory is file cabinet, disk is warehouse.

Draw a flowchart: CPU executing → Interrupt occurs → Save CPU state → Look up interrupt vector → Execute handler → Restore state → Resume execution. Picture it like a phone ringing during a conversation - you pause, answer, then continue.

Imagine two doors: User Door (limited access) and Admin Door (full access). User programs can only go through User Door. Only OS can use Admin Door for privileged operations. This prevents users from accidentally or maliciously damaging the system.

analogies.analogy: Like a traffic control system managing roads (CPU), parking spaces (memory), and intersections (I/O devices) to ensure smooth flow and prevent chaos among competing users.
Like a car with automatic transmission - the driver (user) doesn't need to understand gear ratios, clutch control, or engine timing. The car (OS) abstracts this complexity.
Like a receptionist at an information desk - user programs cannot directly access the manager (kernel). They must request services through the receptionist (system call interface).
Like a fire alarm in an office - when it sounds (interrupt), everyone stops work, emergency procedures run (handler), then normal work resumes. The alarm doesn't wait for current task to finish.
Like a hotel concierge managing all guest requests and coordinating with different hotel departments - translating requests into actions and managing the flow of information.
